triage_reasoning: SRC (p60-Src) is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase that resides on the cytoplasmic face of the plasma membrane. It is myristoylated at Gly2 and further palmitoylated, anchoring it to the inner leaflet of the PM. The kinase domain, SH2, and SH3 domains all face the cytoplasm. There is no extracellular domain. Checking contextual buckets: (1) cell_state_induced — no evidence of stress/activation flipping SRC to outer leaflet; (2) tissue_restricted_surface — not applicable, inner-leaflet anchor; (3) lysosomal_exocytosis — not a lysosomal TM protein; (4) dual_localization — SRC cycles among PM inner leaflet, endosomes, and perinuclear membranes, all cytoplasmic-face; (5) stable_surface_attachment — no ectodomain or secreted form that anchors externally. No antibody/ADC/CAR-T programs target the SRC protein body on the cell surface. The protein body is entirely inaccessible from the extracellular space.

executive_summary.one_paragraph: SRC is canonically an inner-leaflet myristoylated kinase with no extracellular domain, but two independent 2025 publications (PMID:41818370, PMID:41818382) report that in cancer cells autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) topologically inverts SRC onto the outer plasma membrane leaflet, creating an extracellular membrane-associated form (eSrc). Anti-eSrc antibodies mediate tumor killing in vitro and in xenograft models, providing functional proof of extracellular accessibility in the cancer state. Surface exposure is strictly cancer-state-dependent; normal cells show only canonical inner-leaflet localization.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: SRC presents a genuine biological conflict between canonical and cancer-specific topology. Strong contradicting evidence: SRC-family kinases are canonically tethered via acyl groups to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane with all functional domains cytoplasmic (a1_evi_06, high-weight); functional assays show SRC acting at the PM inner leaflet (a1_evi_14, moderate); and CSC methodology analysis confirms SRC lacks N-glycosylation and would not be directly captured by surface glycoprotein enrichment (a1_evi_09, a1_evi_15, moderate). Supporting surface access: two independent sources (PMID:41818370 and PMID:41818382) report noncanonical ALE-mediated translocation and topological inversion in cancer (a1_evi_01–05, moderate), and anti-Src antibody-mediated tumor killing in xenografts constitutes high-weight functional evidence of extracellular accessibility in cancer (a1_evi_03, high). The conflict is real: canonical inner-leaflet localization is well-established, but a cancer-specific surface-exposed form is reported from two independent sources with therapeutic antibody validation. Grade: conflicting.

Topological inversion claim via N-myristoylation in cancer; same single source, functional assay context
Independent corroborating source (PMID:41818382) confirming exocytosis-based Src surface exposure in cancer
Canonical inner-leaflet topology; SRC-family kinases tethered via acyl groups, all domains cytoplasmic, no extracellular domain

Reagent list for surface biotinylation assay for SRC detection; implies surface biotinylation experiment was performed (PMC9659096)

expression_observations.context: Cancer cell lines (in vitro) — anti-Src antibody-based therapy mediates tumor cell killing
Primary human tumors — eSrc detected in primary tumor tissue
Mouse xenograft models — anti-Src antibody mediates tumor cell killing in vivo
Cancer cells — exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface, framed as therapeutic targeting opportunity

Embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells — SRC detected in surfaceome cluster 2 via sialoglycoprotein enrichment LC-MS/MS; co-purification artefact suspected as SRC lacks extracellular N-glycosylation sites
Unspecified species/cell type — SRC kinase appeared in a surfaceome functional cluster alongside ERBB2, CSF1R, PRKCD; framed as non-receptor signaling kinase, possible co-capture artefact
High-invadopodia-activity human cancer cell lines — SRC identified among proteins with greater abundance in secreted small extracellular vesicles (sEVs), alongside CTTN, CFL1, ITGA3, ITGB3

HaCaT keratinocytes — whole-cell lysate western blot for total SRC and phospho-SRC (Y416); no surface fractionation or biotinylation step; expression-level evidence only

non_surface_expression.context: SRC and phospho-Y416-SRC whole-cell lysate detection in HaCaT keratinocytes (no surface fractionation)

therapeutic_engagement.description: Anti-Src antibody-based therapies targeting extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) have demonstrated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models (PMID:41818370). No named antibody, ADC, or sponsor is specified in the ledger. The therapeutic rationale is grounded in the cancer-specific noncanonical surface translocation of Src via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE), which exposes the kinase on the outer leaflet of cancer cell plasma membranes.
therapeutic_engagement.surface_form_rationale: The drug-engaged form is the cancer-specific 'eSrc' — a topologically inverted, membrane-anchored Src present on the outer leaflet of cancer cell plasma membranes following ALE-mediated exocytosis. Canonical Src is an inner-leaflet cytoplasmic kinase with no extracellular domain and is not accessible to antibody therapeutics under normal conditions. Only the inverted eSrc form, documented in cancer cells in vitro and in primary tumors in vivo, constitutes the surface-accessible therapeutic target.

contradicting_evidence.claim: SRC is canonically anchored via myristoylation/palmitoylation to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane, with all functional domains (kinase, SH2, SH3) facing the cytoplasm and no extracellular domain present. This canonical topology directly refutes surface accessibility under normal conditions. Supporting functional data in MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells further confirms that SRC's role at the plasma membrane is as an intracellular kinase (evidenced by pY416 activation state and inner-leaflet membrane fractionation), not as a surface-exposed protein.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: SRC is a peripheral membrane protein attached to the cytoplasmic face via lipid anchors; it has no transmembrane domain and no extracellular segment. Any apparent surface signal in cancer or aberrant contexts would need to be reconciled with non-canonical mechanisms (e.g., exosomal display, unconventional secretion, or necrotic exposure) rather than normal topology. The MC3T3-E1 shear-stress data reinforces the inner-leaflet localization even under stimulated conditions.

cell_states.descriptor: In cancer cells (in vitro and in vivo), SRC undergoes topological inversion via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE), exposing it on the outer plasma membrane leaflet as extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc); absent in normal cells.
Fluid shear stress increases c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) in osteoblast-like cells, linking mechanical stimulation to Src kinase activity.

Cancer cell lines with high invadopodia activity show enrichment of SRC in secreted small extracellular vesicles (sEVs), indicating state-dependent redistribution toward the extracellular compartment.

dual_localization.condition: in cancer cells; mediated by autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE)
in invasive cancer cell lines with high invadopodia activity
canonical localization via N-myristoylation; in non-cancer cells

anatomical_accessibility.rationale: In cancer cells, SRC undergoes topological inversion via autophagolysosomal exocytosis, exposing the kinase domain on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane. This extracellular eSrc is accessible to systemically delivered antibodies, as confirmed by antibody-mediated tumor killing in vitro and in xenograft models.

anatomical_accessibility.context: normal (non-cancer) cells — canonical inner leaflet localization
anatomical_accessibility.rationale: In normal cells SRC is N-myristoylated and anchored to the cytoplasmic inner leaflet, with no extracellular domain exposed. Systemically delivered binders cannot reach it in this baseline state; surface exposure is strictly cancer-state-dependent.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal (non-cancer) cells where SRC is canonically inner-leaflet-anchored and not accessible from the extracellular space
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cells (in vitro cell lines and primary tumors in vivo) where SRC undergoes topological inversion onto the outer cell surface
accessibility_modulation.change: N-myristoylated SRC, normally confined to the cytoplasmic inner leaflet, is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the extracellular outer leaflet specifically in malignant cells, creating a surface-exposed form (eSrc) absent in normal cells.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: eSrc on the outer membrane of cancer cells is accessible to extracellular antibodies; anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediate tumor cell killing in culture and xenograft models, making eSrc a cancer-selective therapeutic target.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal cells lacking prominent autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) activity; SRC remains intracellular
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cell lines with active autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) pathway
accessibility_modulation.change: ALE serves as the secretory mechanism that delivers intracellular SRC to the outer cell surface in cancer cells, routing SRC through autophagolysosomal compartments to achieve extracellular membrane exposure.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Dependence on ALE means surface SRC exposure is mechanistically tied to lysosomal fusion activity; this pathway is prominent in cancer cells, explaining cancer-selective extracellular SRC accessibility for binder engagement.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Non-cancer cells where SRC is exclusively cytoplasmic/inner-leaflet-associated
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cells exhibiting both intracellular SRC (cytoplasmic kinase pool) and extracellular membrane-associated eSrc generated via autophagolysosomal exocytosis
accessibility_modulation.change: In cancer cells SRC occupies two topologically distinct compartments simultaneously: the canonical inner leaflet/cytoplasmic pool and a newly surface-exposed outer-leaflet pool (eSrc), creating a dual-localization state.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: The outer-leaflet eSrc pool is directly accessible to extracellular binders without membrane permeabilization, whereas the cytoplasmic pool remains inaccessible; therapeutic antibodies can selectively engage only the eSrc fraction.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Resting osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells without fluid shear stress stimulation
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Fluid shear stress-stimulated MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells with elevated c-Src Tyr416 phosphorylation
accessibility_modulation.change: Fluid shear stress increases c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery and membrane fraction, indicating mechanically induced remodeling of Src-dependent surface protein distribution.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Mechanical activation of Src in osteoblasts modulates surface protein composition at the plasma membrane; while direct eSrc surface exposure is not documented here, the state-dependent Src activity shift may influence accessibility of Src-regulated surface targets in bone cell contexts.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (whole-protein pLDDT — no ECD residues in topology)

surface_bind.attribution: © Marchand, Khakzad, Correia et al. — EPFL / Inria / Novo Nordisk

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: SRC membrane anchoring in the cancer-state eSrc form is driven by N-myristoylation and ALE-mediated exocytosis; no obligate co-receptor is required for the topological inversion or outer-leaflet membrane association. The ALE pathway itself is the enabling mechanism, not a protein co-receptor.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: No evidence in the ledger restricts eSrc to a specific plasma membrane subdomain (apical, junctional, etc.). The ALE-mediated outer-leaflet exposure appears distributed across the cancer cell surface based on antibody-killing assay data.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: Canonical SRC has no extracellular domain (ecd_length_residues = 0); it is an inner-leaflet lipid-anchored kinase. In the eSrc cancer state the topologically inverted kinase body faces the extracellular space, but this is not a conventional ECD. Antibody engagement targets the inverted kinase domain rather than a dedicated ECD.

epitope_masking.rationale: In the eSrc form the topologically inverted kinase domain faces extracellularly, but the myristoyl anchor remains membrane-embedded and portions of the kinase domain may be occluded by the membrane or by intramolecular regulatory interactions (SH2/SH3 contacts). No direct epitope-mapping data exist in the ledger; moderate conformational masking is inferred.

evidence.claim: Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer cells, both in vitro and in vivo, constituting a surface-expression event for what is canonically an inner-leaflet kinase. This is the primary claim of the paper (PMID 41818370) and represents the core surface-accessibility assertion for SRC.

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) is identified as the secretory mechanism by which Src is transported to the cell surface in cancer cell lines; this noncanonical pathway rationalises how an N-myristoylated cytoplasmic kinase reaches the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane.

spans.quote: We identified autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) as a secretory mechanism prominent in cancer cell lines.

evidence.claim: Extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) is detected in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediate tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models. This constitutes direct therapeutic engagement evidence: an antibody program targeting the extracellular face of Src on cancer cells (preclinical stage, in vitro + xenograft in vivo), demonstrating that eSrc is accessible to antibody therapeutics from the extracellular space.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: primary tumors and cancer cell lines; mouse xenograft

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: SRC is topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer via its N-myristoylation motif: the canonical inner-leaflet N-myristoylated anchor is retained, but the orientation is flipped so that the protein body faces the extracellular space. This is a topology claim specific to the cancer-associated eSrc form.

spans.quote: Thus, intracellular N-myristoylated proteins, prototypically Src, can be topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer and targeted with antibody therapeutics.

evidence.claim: A second independent paper (PMID 41818382) corroborates that exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, explicitly framing this as a therapeutic targeting opportunity. This is a cross-source replication of the eSrc surface-expression finding, supporting accessibility of Src on the extracellular face in cancer.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: SRC-family kinases, including SRC, are canonically tethered via acyl groups (myristoylation/palmitoylation) to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane. All functional domains (kinase, SH2, SH3) face the cytoplasm and there is no extracellular domain. This is the canonical topology that directly refutes surface accessibility under normal (non-cancer) conditions.

spans.quote: These motifs are phosphorylated by SRC-family tyrosine kinases, which are tethered via acyl groups to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane.

evidence.claim: SRC kinase was identified in a surfaceome characterization study alongside well-known receptor and non-receptor signaling kinases (ERBB2, CSF1R, PRKCD), but it is framed as a non-receptor signaling kinase within a functional cluster rather than as a confirmed surface protein by direct surfaceome capture. This is an ambiguous result: SRC appears in a surfaceome dataset context but may reflect co-capture or indirect enrichment rather than direct PM glycoprotein capture.

spans.quote: The ephrin/plexin group was part of a larger functional cluster with roles in chemokine signaling and increased chemotaxis 34 , 35 that included not just well-known receptor/non-receptor signaling kinases (e.g., ERBB2, CSF1R, SRC, PRKCD), but also proteins with functionally diverse activities (i.e., proteins with roles in immune response, cell adhesion cell-ECM binding molecules, and transport).

evidence.claim: SRC kinase was detected in surfaceome cluster 2 via LC-MS/MS sialoglycoprotein-enrichment cell-surface capture in embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells during differentiation. SRC is listed alongside bona fide surface proteins (RECK, FGFR2, ephrin receptors, GPC1), but its assignment to the surfaceome cluster may reflect co-purification of an inner-leaflet kinase with glycoprotein complexes rather than direct N-glycan-based capture.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells

spans.quote: Surfaceome cluster 2 harbors several cell-surface regulators of chondrocyte biology, including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1

evidence.claim: The surfaceome study in chondrogenic cells used sialoglycoprotein enrichment via aminooxy-biotin conjugation followed by high-resolution LC-MS/MS, conducted across chondrogenic differentiation timepoints on 3 biological replicates. This method is specific for N-glycosylated extracellular-domain proteins; SRC lacks N-glycosylation sites and no extracellular domain, making its detection in this assay methodologically suspect (co-enrichment artefact).

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells

spans.quote: Temporal proteomic profiling was conducted at days 1, 3, 6, 10, and 15 of micromass culture corresponding to key histodifferentiation stages [ 17 , 18 ], combining sialoglycoprotein enrichment via aminooxy-biotin conjugation with high-resolution LC-MS/MS on 3 biological replicates ( n = 3).

evidence.claim: SRC was identified among proteins with greater abundance in secreted small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) from high-invadopodia-activity cancer cell lines, alongside CTTN, CFL1, ITGA3, ITGB3. This represents a shed/secreted form of SRC packaged into exosomes/sEVs, constituting a potential risk factor for interpretation of surface-accessibility assays (sEV-associated SRC could confound PM-anchored SRC detection).

spans.quote: Furthermore, these high invadopodia activity cell lines secreted sEVs with a greater abundance of proteins involved in the regulation of invadopodia formation (CTTN, CFL1, SRC, ITGA3, ITGB3 – Cluster 2) and proteolytic activity (MMP2, MMP14, BSG/CD147 – Cluster 2) (Fig. 3 D).

evidence.claim: Co-immunoprecipitation using anti-THY1 antibody demonstrates a physical association between SRC and THY1 (a GPI-anchored surface protein) in HONE1-VA, HONE1-THY1, and NP460 cells. This association with a bona fide surface protein could contribute to epitope-masking or could co-tether SRC to the inner leaflet at PM-proximal microdomains, but does not itself demonstrate extracellular accessibility of SRC.

spans.quote: Co-IP analysis of SRC and THY1 using anti-THY1 was performed in ( C ) HONE1-VA, HONE1-THY1, and ( D ) NP460 cells.

evidence.claim: Western blot of total SRC and phospho-SRC (Y416) in HaCaT keratinocytes without subcellular fractionation or surface biotinylation step; this is a whole-cell lysate WB with no surface isolation, providing only whole-cell protein-level evidence and not surface localization evidence for SRC.

spans.quote: n = 2, representative pictures. b qRT-PCR analyses of CYP1A1 and AKR1C3 in HaCaT-shAHR and HaCaT-EV cells treated with 10 μM PP2 or 0.1 % DMSO for 24 h. n = 4. *, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV DMSO, #, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV B[a]P. c qRT-PCR analyses of CYP1A1 and AKR1C3 in HaCaT-shAHR and HaCaT-EV keratinocytes treated as indicated for 24 h. n = 4–7. *, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV DMSO, #, p ≤ 0.05 compared to EV B[a]P. d NHEKs were treated with 20 ng/ml AREG for 24 h and AKR1C3 transcript level were analyzed by qRT-PCR. e Western blot analyses of SRC and its phosphorylated form (Y416).

evidence.claim: Antibody identifiers for anti-SRC detection in cell-surface biotinylation experiments: mouse monoclonal anti-SRC clone 60315-1-lg (Proteintech, Rosemont, IL) and rabbit anti-phospho-Y416-SRC clone D49G4 (#6943, Cell Signaling Technology, Danvers, MA). These are the reagents associated with the SRC detection in the surface biotinylation assay described in the same paper (PMC9659096).

spans.quote: mouse monoclonal anti-PCNA (NA03, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany); rabbit anti-GLUT1 (ab652; Abcam, Cambridge, UK); goat anti-GLUT1 (sc-1603; Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Santa Cruz, CA, USA); mouse monoclonal anti-RAC1 (05-389, Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA); mouse monoclonal anti-SRC antibody (60315-1-lg, Proteintech, Rosemont, IL, USA); rabbit anti-phospho Y416-SRC antibody D49G4 (#6943, Cell Signaling, Danvers, MA, USA); mouse monoclonal anti-VAV2 antibody (sc-271442, Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Santa Cruz, CA, USA)

evidence.claim: Fluid shear stress increases c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery with an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction in osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells. This is evidence of c-Src activation state measured by phospho-Y416 WB and subcellular fractionation, demonstrating SRC's role at the PM inner leaflet under mechanical stimulation. SRC itself is not shown at the cell surface; it is the activating kinase for membrane-directed RANKL trafficking.

spans.quote: Fluid shear stress increased c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promoted redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery, accompanied by an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction.

evidence.claim: Cell surface capture (CSC) methodology: periodate-oxidized sialic acids on cell-surface glycoproteins react with hydrazide beads to form stable hydrazone linkages; captured glycoproteins are washed and released by PNGase F. This is the methodological basis for the surfaceome MS studies cited in A1 surface-evidence records. SRC lacks an extracellular N-glycosylated domain and would not be expected to be directly captured by this method; any SRC detection in CSC datasets would require scrutiny as co-purification artefact.

spans.quote: The aldehydes readily react with hydrazide, to form stable hydrazone linkages. 75 The enrichment technique called cell surface capture (CSC) developed by Wollscheid, Aebersold, and co-workers, utilized this chemistry to capture cell surface proteins onto hydrazide beads. 76 The captured glycoproteins were washed extensively and released using PNGase F, an amidase that cleaves between the innermost GlcNAc and the N -glycan-modified asparagine.

evidence.claim: A second results clip from PMC12777226 explicitly names SRC kinase in the surfaceome cluster alongside RECK, FGFR2, ephrin receptors, and GPC1, with an annotation of 'chondrocyte phenotype control'. This corroborates the cluster assignment result but is a near-duplicate of draft_PMC12777226_results_01; however, it provides the additional functional annotation context that may inform downstream interpretation of this ambiguous finding.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells

spans.quote: including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1; a proteoglycan and cell surface co-receptor involved in growth factor pathway modulation [ 49 ]), highlighting surfaceome mechanisms in cartilage development and regeneration.

evidence.claim: Src protein is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the outer cell surface in cancer cells, both in vitro and in vivo, representing a disease-state-induced surface accessibility event. This contradicts the canonical view of SRC as exclusively inner-leaflet-anchored.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: cancer cell lines and primary tumors

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) is identified as the secretory mechanism responsible for exposing Src on the outer cell surface in cancer cell lines. This establishes the mechanistic route by which intracellular SRC reaches the extracellular leaflet in the cancer cell state.

spans.quote: We identified autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) as a secretory mechanism prominent in cancer cell lines.

evidence.claim: Extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) is detected in primary tumors in vivo. Anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture and mouse xenograft models, confirming accessible extracellular Src protein on the cancer cell surface in disease context.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: primary tumor tissue and xenograft models

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: N-myristoylated Src, prototypically an inner-leaflet-anchored cytoplasmic kinase, undergoes topological inversion onto the cell surface specifically in cancer. This defines a cancer-state-specific accessibility modulation: SRC surface exposure is absent in normal cells but present at the outer membrane in malignant cells.

spans.quote: Thus, intracellular N-myristoylated proteins, prototypically Src, can be topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer and targeted with antibody therapeutics.

evidence.claim: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer cell surface specifically in cancer cells, positioning it as a therapeutically targetable antigen. Baseline state (normal/non-cancer cells): SRC is inner-leaflet-anchored and not surface-accessible. Cancer cell state: surface-exposed eSrc detectable on outer leaflet.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: In osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells, c-Src (the mouse ortholog of SRC) is involved in mechanosensing: fluid shear stress modulates RANKL subcellular distribution. SRC is expressed and functionally active in osteoblast-lineage cells, consistent with the high Src expression known in bone-resorbing cells.

spans.quote: Here, we tested whether fluid shear stress alters RANKL subcellular distribution in osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells and examined the involvement of the non-receptor tyrosine kinase c-Src.

evidence.claim: Fluid shear stress increases c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) in osteoblast-like cells, linking mechanical stimulation to Src kinase activity in the bone cell context. This documents a cell-state (mechanically activated) regulation of Src activity in osteoblast-lineage cells.

spans.quote: Fluid shear stress increased c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promoted redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery, accompanied by an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction.

evidence.claim: SRC (c-Src) is functionally expressed in thyroid follicular cells and participates in the SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN pathway that regulates NIS actin-cytoskeleton anchoring and retention at the plasma membrane. This documents SRC's role in modulating surface protein retention in thyroid epithelial cell biology.

spans.quote: Biochemical and functional analysis allowed us to establish the SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN pathway as a key regulator of NIS actin-cytoskeleton anchoring and retention at the PM [ 10 ].

evidence.claim: SRC is among the proteins enriched in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) secreted by cancer cell lines with high invadopodia activity, suggesting a cell-state-dependent (high-invadopodia vs. low-invadopodia) redistribution of SRC toward the extracellular compartment in invasive cancer states.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: invasive cancer cell lines (high invadopodia activity)

spans.quote: Furthermore, these high invadopodia activity cell lines secreted sEVs with a greater abundance of proteins involved in the regulation of invadopodia formation (CTTN, CFL1, SRC, ITGA3, ITGB3 – Cluster 2) and proteolytic activity (MMP2, MMP14, BSG/CD147 – Cluster 2) (Fig. 3 D).

evidence.claim: SRC kinase is detected in the surfaceome cluster of chondrocytes (chondrocyte phenotype control cluster), indicating SRC expression in chondrocytes is associated with cell-surface regulatory programs in cartilage biology.

spans.quote: Surfaceome cluster 2 harbors several cell-surface regulators of chondrocyte biology, including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1

confidence_reasoning: Confidence is moderate because the cancer-cell outer-leaflet SRC story rests on a single recent research cluster: two 2025 papers (PMID:41818370 and PMID:41818382) reporting topological inversion via autophagolysosomal exocytosis. These two publications appear to originate from the same or closely related groups, and no independent replication with orthogonal methodology has been reported in the ledger. The canonical inner-leaflet topology — N-myristoylated, all domains cytoplasmic, no extracellular domain — is established across decades of independent structural and cell-biology work. Lifting confidence to high would require a third independent group confirming outer-leaflet eSrc exposure in cancer cells using complementary methods (e.g., cryo-EM topology mapping, protease-protection assay in intact cells, or a separately sponsored antibody program reaching clinical stage).
